claims.claim_text: The opening payment-outage vignette presents a specific unsourced incident narrative with false-precise operational details and a quantitative customer-impact claim.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: A payment team ships a small memory optimization late on a Thursday. At first the dashboards look clean, then checkout latency creeps upward, queue depth rises, and pods begin restarting in one availability zone. The on-call engineer is asleep, the runbook exists in a wiki, and the first alert says only that error rate crossed a threshold. By the time a human connects the deploy, the memory leak, the pod churn, and the rising backlog, customers have already retried thousands of failed purchases.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A service can show a familiar failure pattern after a small change: dashboards stay quiet at first, then latency rises, queues deepen, pods restart, and customer-facing errors appear before a human fully connects the symptoms.
claims.rationale: This reads like a concrete outage story but provides no attributable real incident source, so the safe fix is to keep the lesson and remove the fabricated specifics.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes self-healing controllers replace failed Pods to maintain desired state, and Services stop routing to failed Pods.
claims.span_hint: section: From Incident Signal to Decision Mode table; section: Safe Auto-Remediation Principles; section: Did You Know bullet 1
claims.rationale: These sections rely on concrete Kubernetes self-healing behavior that is directly documented upstream.

claims.claim_text: CronJobs create Jobs on a repeating schedule, and Kubernetes advises that CronJob-created Jobs be idempotent because scheduling is approximate.
claims.rationale: The implementation-pattern comparison makes a concrete claim about CronJob behavior that the Kubernetes CronJob documentation states explicitly.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes RBAC RoleBindings grant permissions within a specific namespace, and the documented most-secure pattern is to grant roles to application-specific ServiceAccounts.
claims.span_hint: section: Kubernetes Implementation Patterns, RBAC paragraph
claims.rationale: The module makes a concrete RBAC-scoping and least-privilege claim that is directly covered by the upstream RBAC reference.

claims.claim_text: `CrashLoopBackOff` can appear in kubectl status output when a Pod repeatedly fails to start.
claims.span_hint: section: The Auto-Remediation Contract; section: Building Runbooks That Machines and Humans Can Share
claims.rationale: This is a specific Kubernetes status-term claim and should point to the Pod lifecycle documentation.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes supports restarting a Deployment with `kubectl rollout restart`.
claims.span_hint: section: Common action classification table; section: The Auto-Remediation Contract
claims.rationale: The module names a concrete kubectl operation, so the command should be backed by the kubectl reference.

claims.claim_text: Deployment rollouts replace Pods at a controlled rate, and manually deleting a Pod managed by a Deployment causes an immediate replacement to be created by the ReplicaSet.
claims.span_hint: section: The Auto-Remediation Contract; section: From Incident Signal to Decision Mode table; section: Guardrails, Rollback, and Verification in Practice
claims.rationale: These remediation examples depend on documented Deployment and ReplicaSet behavior rather than general pedagogy.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Self-Healing
further_reading.why_relevant: It frames which recovery behaviors Kubernetes already provides before teams add higher-level remediation logic.

further_reading.title: Using RBAC Authorization
further_reading.why_relevant: It is the primary upstream reference for namespace scoping, ServiceAccount permissions, and least-privilege remediation identities.

further_reading.title: CronJob
further_reading.why_relevant: It documents the polling, scheduling, concurrency, and idempotency constraints that matter when a remediator is implemented as a CronJob.

notes: No allowlist expansion was needed. Exercise steps, quiz scenarios, mermaid diagrams, and code blocks were excluded unless surrounding prose made a reusable factual Kubernetes claim.
